title: Launching Health in ChatGPT to US Users
excerpt: I think doctors are going to lose their minds or at the very least get extremely agitated/frustrated as patients increasingly use AI tools to do self-research for symptoms and diagnosis. And I know this because I am guilty of this myself and recently had a dentist tell me to not do that. And this applies to so many health workers, your GP as well as your physical therapist, etc.
content_html:
I think doctors are going to lose their minds or at the very least get extremely agitated/frustrated as patients increasingly use AI tools to do self-research for symptoms and diagnosis. And I know this because I am guilty of this myself and recently had a dentist tell me to not do that. And this applies to so many health workers, your GP as well as your physical therapist, etc.

Recently I had Claude analyze an MRI of my knee and it gave me five different potential problems, but once I got the radiologist report - none of those problems were present, it did not detect that it was chondromalacia that I found out from the radiologist report.

Health and the current iteration of LLM's just doesn't seem like a great match.


I don’t have the reference on my fingertips, but patients who are involved in their own healthcare have better outcomes. Moreover, for those of us (and there are many of us) who have gotten increasingly frustrated with US healthcare (e.g., long waits, short visits where you never really get a chance to discuss the health issue, difficulty getting appropriate tests), AI is looking like an increasingly attractive option. Not as a replacement for physicians, but at least as a path forward to possibly better understand what is going on.


I like it when patients research things. Consult is more engaging for me when people care about and try to understand stuff.

It's hard for patients to interpret medical research and evidence though... ChatGPT recommended treatment for some of my family members based on studies with 60 patients. It wasn't exactly established medical practice.


I was poking around with some symptoms into Gemini not too seriously. It suggested a diagnosis which I have since forgotten. I looked it up and it seems like something with less than 100 known cases in human history.

I think someone needs to teach these models the old "horses not zebras" adage, or we're gonna have a lot of people insisting on having rare cancers.


There is a fine line between being a well-informed and engaged patient and the whole “dO yOuR oWn ReSeArCh” madness. A lot of people don’t know where that line is and when they’ve crossed over it.


Radiology is probably an edge case, due to needing the right inductive bias and training data that general purpose multimodal language models may lack. At least until someone creates RadiologyBench and provides an incentive to the labs to make that part of their data pipeline.

I have witnessed a non-radiology case (a STD on the face) where the LLM got it right and the doctor didn't (he said it was diabetes related). It may have saved that person's life by causing them to go to a different doctor with the LLM's diagnosis and getting the proper treatment.

What I don't understand is why don't doctors use LLMs as a tool for a quick second opinion before arriving at a determination? They have my data. They can put it into GPT 5.6 Pro or Fable, read the output, and choose to disregard it if they really want to, but if there's a 20% chance that it spots something they missed, why not?


Yes. The issue is the the average user doesn't understand that the vision capabilities of LLMs are limited.

It's a world of difference to upload the radiologist/pathologist report and go from there than uploading the raw images.

The first is a great idea, the second reckless and dangerous.


I have read that there is private software for radiologists that has better training data, but I can’t comment on it myself.

Maybe someone who has used it can give some feedback.


One of the worst things a new mother can do is use google, instant panic attacks. I imagine using an LLM would do the same.

When my first kiddo was an infant any google result for information related to even a sneeze said it was an indication of some super tragic situation. Any advice about a behavior or trait ended with basically "your child is destined to be a serial killer".

For any new parents out there, just call your doctor. Stay far away from the internet and AI when it comes to your baby's health.


I know of someone who had a red bump on their arm and asked ChatGPT if that was a problem and if they should see a doctor. It said no so they left it. Turns out it was a tick and they now have lyme disease.


I have a friend who uploaded a photo of a mark on their back to Claude, which stated it was a cut that was healing. Went to the doctor, it was skin cancer.

That said, Claude did say “I’m not a doctor, if in doubt make sure you see one”. So, that’s good… but then, what’s the point in using it?


Its already happening, I am not a doctor but work with them closely. One patient used ChatGPT for his problems because he waited so long for an appointment. He was a bit neurotic and talked the whole time how one doesnt need a doctor anymore as ChatGPT is already good enough.

The real main problem is that you need to wait to get help, may it be a therapist or an eye doctor. But ChatGPT is instant.

This part worries me the most.


It's not a problem on it's on, it's a symptom of a systemic issue. Access to healthcare can be very limited even in developed countries. Like, you need to sometimes wait half a year to get a specialist appointment in Europe (unless you can afford private healthcare). In this case, people will be using AI to get faster answers.


Theres a good use case in AI for patients to chat to tho, if i could transfer all of the context of a conversation I had with a AI bot to my Dr before a appointment it would most likely improve outcomes because I have been much more verbose and descriptive to the AI bot. You basically want an AI chat bot for patient history...


It's way better than the Google MD problem that previously existed.

I had Claude analyze a blood panel and provide potential diagnoses, medications, dosing and contraindications for a fairly esoteric condition. It gave me the questions to ask a doctor (being completely forthright that I'm yet another layman regurgitating AI output) who agreed with the approach and even admitted that he hadn't seen a case study it referenced but that he wish he knew about sooner.

Will doctors still be assmad that years of their study is now democratized? Probably. I am (or was) a software engineer, welcome to the club.


That study cites that 45% of vendors had hallucinations, likely vendors using smaller or cheaper models. So 55% of vendors had no known hallucinations.


I’m intrigued by the power of these models to help me take more control of my healthcare.

Sure, doctors’ advice is best, but sometimes I wait 6 months to get to see a specialist who seems hell bent on getting through our appt as quickly as possible.

Models are infinitely patient and can see a lot more context about my health.

But, the privacy is a big question mark. The page extensively reassures on privacy (no model training, encrypted, no ads, can be deleted). There’s only one part that stands out to me:

> Memory remains in your control. To personalize future chats, memories can be created from your Health conversations. Memories are not created directly from connected medical records and Apple Health information. If you’d like to have a conversation without creating memories, you can use Temporary Chat (opens in a new window) or turn memory off in Settings > Memory.

Great that you can turn it off in settings but I wonder how many people will do that and how much sensitive context leakage will come out of your conversations and suddenly you’ll get ads for rashes and whatnot.


HIPAA only applies to data originating from a covered entity.

It doesn't apply to information the patient supplies directly.

As a patient, I can take out a street sign displaying my health records if I want. It's my data.

If I want to upload it to a strange AI or any other app, that's completely fine. HIPAA isn't involved, for either me or the company I upload to.

In fact, quite the opposite. CMS has been going to great pains over the past decade or so to create standards to help patients get access to their records and own them themselves.

Different states may have additional protections in this case, and I know there has been some discussion at the federal level about regulating these "wellness" apps.

As an interesting technical detail not many people know, HIPAA only applies to Covered Entities and Business Associates engaging in the electronic exchange of information using "standard transactions", and was intended to regulate insurance and billing transactions.

A doctor that keeps paper charts and bills cash directly to the patient wouldn't fall under HIPAA at all.


It’s a pretty fine line, and if they want to do anything beyond just telling you things about your data, actually taking any sort of actions or communications with health care providers, they should qualify as a business associate under HIPAA. And I certainly am never going to share my healthcare data with an AI company without any sort of regulations or oversight, beyond a TOS.


A Business Associate (BA) under HIPAA is a entity that contracts with a Covered Entity (CE) using a Business Associate Agreement (BAA). OpenAI wouldn't be considered a BA unless they had an executed BAA with a CE.

There are a wide variety of things, including communication with providers, that OpenAI can do without being regulated by HIPAA. The provider, however would most likely be covered, so they would be under an obligation to not disclose any PHI.

As an example, consider a hypothetical "email my doc" app. This app could take health information from the patient and email it to all of their doctors without falling under HIPAA at all.

What you're probably thinking of is the HIPAA Privacy Rule (there's more to HIPAA than just that, the Security Rule is pretty involved). Under the Privacy Rule, CEs and BAs can not disclose PHI without a patient's consent, with a few exceptions.

If the patient is disclosing that information though, all bets are off (aside from state level regulations).


I tried posting an Ask HN about my experience using Claude and ChatGPT for medical advice, but it didn't get enough upvotes for any responses. Maybe this is a good place to repost (the part relevant to medical treatment is at the end):

Question: Why don't Claude or ChatGPT get message timestamps?

Go to any conversation you’ve had with Claude or ChatGPT in their chat apps and ask them what time they sent their last message, and you’ll find they don’t see timestamps for any of the messages in your chat history. They might guess or hallucinate an answer but they’ll be wildly off and if you probe further they should admit they don’t see timestamps at all, they only know the current time. I’ve also noticed generally that if you pick up conversations over multiple days, it doesn’t know that days have passed in between messages.

——

Me: How much time has passed since your last message?

Claude: I don't have a way to measure the gap between my messages—I don't have a clock running between turns, and nothing in our conversation timestamps the messages for me. So I can't tell you how much time has passed since my last reply. Could've been seconds, could've been days.

If it's useful, I can check the current time, but that would only tell you now, not the interval since I last wrote.

——

This is very bizarre to me and seems like an obvious flaw in the product, but both major AI companies are doing this and not implementing the obvious solution of surfacing message timestamps. Does anyone know why they don’t do this?

For an example of the type of issue this causes, I’ve been using Claude for advice while my dog is going through medical treatment and it’s very concerning because I’ll say I gave him some medicine and then later ask questions about a symptom he’s displaying and Claude doesn’t seem to know whether the medicine was minutes ago or days ago and usually doesn’t ask for clarification either. I’ve learned to either be explicit about how much time has passed or just not ask it questions where timing would be a factor. But I’m surprised because this seems like it should be an easy thing to address.


I'm just guessing here, but I find that the models are often quite bad at ignoring information.

So if the harness appended a timestamp to each message the model sees, I wouldn't be surprised if the model often responded with "given that it's 12:30 pm on a Sunday..." even in cases where that's absolutely not relevant.

Maybe you could instead give the model a tool it could call that would return timestamps?


I find this weird too! When you say something that prompts them that you’re talking about the current time, they’ll go and fetch it and use that to understand time frames which makes sense. But if you’ve been talking about something tomorrow, and then tomorrow comes and you go back to that chat, they don’t understand that now it’s today/right now.


The American Medical Association and health insurance industries can all fuck off. The healthcare situation in the US is so absurd and Kafka-esque I welcome LLMs in medicine. I haven’t seen a MD in years, I’ve only seen nurse practitioners and physician assistants. Add to that how insurance will randomly deny tests, procedures and treatments, I have very little use for the current medical establishment.


> I have very little use for the current medical establishment.

What do you think the LLMs are trained on?

I agree the current state of US healthcare is a mess. But that doesn’t mean anything is better. The current state of LLMs are so agreeable I worry what they’ll suggest to users who provide even the slightest pushback.

“Are you sure I need this test?”

“You’re right to question that. It’s unlikely to surface anything you don’t already know”

…while a chronic condition develops under the surface


Agreed on the problems, but how will an LLM fix any of that? Even if it does somehow give you the right answer, it cannot perform procedures or prescribe medicine. You still need to get an appointment for anything beyond information. Your insurance isn't going to say, "Oh, sure, if chatGPT said you need this, we'll approve it."


It sounds like you need a better patient advocate or your area is very underserved in provider choice. Many people have success with the health system.


NYS is trying to make it illegal for LLMs to give medical, legal or engineering advice. Because of course its a state built upon exploitation so.

https://www.nysenate.gov/legislation/bills/2025/S7263/amendm...

The engineering aspect is quite hilarious. I have a PE license, but don't use it. But the PE definition in NY is wide open. Technically, if you do the right work, even writing software can fall under a PE license, though its incredibly rare. So this could theoretically ban LLM Code assistants.


"Coverage is denied for the cost of your visit to the doctor and prescribed treatment because you didn't go through our triage concierge chat bot first."


This is a terrible idea on any way shape or form you can imagine.

Even if they say they won't, they will, and there is no telling what they would do with the data itself...


It's a boon for low income people in low income countries. Some random facts:

- They avoid doctors because it's expensive and they don't want to burden their families.

- There aren't enough doctors, regardless of social spending.

- There isn't enough social spending.

Even if this only increases bandwidth of existing doctors, or allows lesser-trained nurses to cosplay as a doctor during triage to increase bandwidth, it's a great thing.


I bet people out there are already uploading all sorts of private or confidential stuff to ChatGPT. I don't think that will make much of a difference.


This isn't so bad vs the alternative. My healthcare data is spread over a bunch of small systems built with poor security practices at the clinics and hospitals I've been to. It's probably out there in some pirate database already, all cleaned up and consolidated.

With ChatGPT at least I control what I enter in the prompt to minimize my exposure.

Recently I had Claude analyze an MRI of my knee and it gave me five different potential problems, but once I got the radiologist report - none of those problems were present, it did not detect that it was chondromalacia that I found out from the radiologist report.

Health and the current iteration of LLM's just doesn't seem like a great match.

blocks.html_en: I don’t have the reference on my fingertips, but patients who are involved in their own healthcare have better outcomes. Moreover, for those of us (and there are many of us) who have gotten increasingly frustrated with US healthcare (e.g., long waits, short visits where you never really get a chance to discuss the health issue, difficulty getting appropriate tests), AI is looking like an increasingly attractive option. Not as a replacement for physicians, but at least as a path forward to possibly better understand what is going on.

I was poking around with some symptoms into Gemini not too seriously. It suggested a diagnosis which I have since forgotten. I looked it up and it seems like something with less than 100 known cases in human history.

I think someone needs to teach these models the old "horses not zebras" adage, or we're gonna have a lot of people insisting on having rare cancers.

blocks.html_en:
Radiology is probably an edge case, due to needing the right inductive bias and training data that general purpose multimodal language models may lack. At least until someone creates RadiologyBench and provides an incentive to the labs to make that part of their data pipeline.

I have witnessed a non-radiology case (a STD on the face) where the LLM got it right and the doctor didn't (he said it was diabetes related). It may have saved that person's life by causing them to go to a different doctor with the LLM's diagnosis and getting the proper treatment.

What I don't understand is why don't doctors use LLMs as a tool for a quick second opinion before arriving at a determination? They have my data. They can put it into GPT 5.6 Pro or Fable, read the output, and choose to disregard it if they really want to, but if there's a 20% chance that it spots something they missed, why not?

sentences.en: Radiology is probably an edge case, due to needing the right inductive bias and training data that general purpose multimodal language models may lack.
sentences.zh: 放射学可能是一种边缘情况，因为需要通用多模式语言模型可能缺乏的正确归纳偏差和训练数据。

sentences.zh: 至少在有人创建 RadiologyBench 并激励实验室将其纳入其数据管道之前。我亲眼目睹了一个非放射学病例（面部性传播疾病），法学硕士做对了，但医生没有做对（他说这与糖尿病有关）。

blocks.html_en:
One of the worst things a new mother can do is use google, instant panic attacks. I imagine using an LLM would do the same.

When my first kiddo was an infant any google result for information related to even a sneeze said it was an indication of some super tragic situation. Any advice about a behavior or trait ended with basically "your child is destined to be a serial killer".

For any new parents out there, just call your doctor. Stay far away from the internet and AI when it comes to your baby's health.

sentences.en: One of the worst things a new mother can do is use google, instant panic attacks.
sentences.zh: 新妈妈能做的最糟糕的事情之一就是使用谷歌，瞬间恐慌发作。

sentences.en: I imagine using an LLM would do the same.When my first kiddo was an infant any google result for information related to even a sneeze said it was an indication of some super tragic situation.
sentences.zh: 我想使用法学硕士也会做同样的事情。当我的第一个孩子还是婴儿时，任何与打喷嚏相关的信息的谷歌搜索结果都表明这预示着某种超级悲惨的情况。

sentences.en: Any advice about a behavior or trait ended with basically "your child is destined to be a serial killer".For any new parents out there, just call your doctor.
sentences.zh: 任何关于行为或特质的建议基本上都会以“你的孩子注定成为连环杀手”结束。对于任何新父母来说，只需打电话给你的医生即可。

sentences.en: Stay far away from the internet and AI when it comes to your baby's health.
sentences.zh: 为了宝宝的健康，请远离互联网和人工智能。

blocks.html_en:
Its already happening, I am not a doctor but work with them closely. One patient used ChatGPT for his problems because he waited so long for an appointment. He was a bit neurotic and talked the whole time how one doesnt need a doctor anymore as ChatGPT is already good enough.

The real main problem is that you need to wait to get help, may it be a therapist or an eye doctor. But ChatGPT is instant.

This part worries me the most.

sentences.zh: 它已经发生了，我不是医生，但与他们密切合作。

sentences.zh: 一名患者使用 ChatGPT 来解决他的问题，因为他等待预约的时间太长了。

sentences.zh: 但ChatGPT是即时的。这部分是我最担心的。

blocks.html_en: It's not a problem on it's on, it's a symptom of a systemic issue. Access to healthcare can be very limited even in developed countries. Like, you need to sometimes wait half a year to get a specialist appointment in Europe (unless you can afford private healthcare). In this case, people will be using AI to get faster answers.

sentences.en: Access to healthcare can be very limited even in developed countries.
sentences.zh: 即使在发达国家，获得医疗保健的机会也非常有限。

sentences.en: Like, you need to sometimes wait half a year to get a specialist appointment in Europe (unless you can afford private healthcare).
sentences.zh: 例如，您有时需要等待半年才能在欧洲获得专家预约（除非您负担得起私人医疗保健费用）。

sentences.en: In this case, people will be using AI to get faster answers.
sentences.zh: 在这种情况下，人们将使用人工智能来获得更快的答案。

It gave me the questions to ask a doctor (being completely forthright that I'm yet another layman regurgitating AI output) who agreed with the approach and even admitted that he hadn't seen a case study it referenced but that he wish he knew about sooner.

Will doctors still be assmad that years of their study is now democratized? Probably. I am (or was) a software engineer, welcome to the club.

blocks.html_en:
I’m intrigued by the power of these models to help me take more control of my healthcare.

Sure, doctors’ advice is best, but sometimes I wait 6 months to get to see a specialist who seems hell bent on getting through our appt as quickly as possible.

Models are infinitely patient and can see a lot more context about my health.

But, the privacy is a big question mark. The page extensively reassures on privacy (no model training, encrypted, no ads, can be deleted). There’s only one part that stands out to me:

> Memory remains in your control. To personalize future chats, memories can be created from your Health conversations. Memories are not created directly from connected medical records and Apple Health information. If you’d like to have a conversation without creating memories, you can use Temporary Chat (opens in a new window) or turn memory off in Settings > Memory.

Great that you can turn it off in settings but I wonder how many people will do that and how much sensitive context leakage will come out of your conversations and suddenly you’ll get ads for rashes and whatnot.

blocks.html_en:
HIPAA only applies to data originating from a covered entity.

It doesn't apply to information the patient supplies directly.

As a patient, I can take out a street sign displaying my health records if I want. It's my data.

If I want to upload it to a strange AI or any other app, that's completely fine. HIPAA isn't involved, for either me or the company I upload to.

In fact, quite the opposite. CMS has been going to great pains over the past decade or so to create standards to help patients get access to their records and own them themselves.

Different states may have additional protections in this case, and I know there has been some discussion at the federal level about regulating these "wellness" apps.

As an interesting technical detail not many people know, HIPAA only applies to Covered Entities and Business Associates engaging in the electronic exchange of information using "standard transactions", and was intended to regulate insurance and billing transactions.

A doctor that keeps paper charts and bills cash directly to the patient wouldn't fall under HIPAA at all.

sentences.zh: HIPAA 仅适用于源自受保护实体的数据。它不适用于患者直接提供的信息。作为患者，如果我愿意，我可以拿出显示我的健康记录的街道标志。

sentences.zh: 这是我的数据。如果我想将其上传到一个奇怪的人工智能或任何其他应用程序，那完全没问题。

sentences.zh: 无论是对于我还是我上传到的公司来说，HIPAA 都不涉及。事实上，恰恰相反。

sentences.zh: 在过去十年左右的时间里，CMS 一直在煞费苦心地制定标准，以帮助患者访问他们的记录并自己拥有这些记录。在这种情况下，不同的州可能有额外的保护，我知道在联邦层面已经有一些关于监管这些“健康”应用程序的讨论。作为一个有趣的技术细节，没有多少人知道，HIPAA 仅适用于使用“标准交易”进行电子信息交换的涵盖实体和商业伙伴，旨在监管保险和计费交易。将纸质图表和账单直接交给患者现金根本不属于 HIPAA 的范围。

blocks.html_en:
A Business Associate (BA) under HIPAA is a entity that contracts with a Covered Entity (CE) using a Business Associate Agreement (BAA). OpenAI wouldn't be considered a BA unless they had an executed BAA with a CE.

There are a wide variety of things, including communication with providers, that OpenAI can do without being regulated by HIPAA. The provider, however would most likely be covered, so they would be under an obligation to not disclose any PHI.

As an example, consider a hypothetical "email my doc" app. This app could take health information from the patient and email it to all of their doctors without falling under HIPAA at all.

What you're probably thinking of is the HIPAA Privacy Rule (there's more to HIPAA than just that, the Security Rule is pretty involved). Under the Privacy Rule, CEs and BAs can not disclose PHI without a patient's consent, with a few exceptions.

If the patient is disclosing that information though, all bets are off (aside from state level regulations).

blocks.html_en:
I tried posting an Ask HN about my experience using Claude and ChatGPT for medical advice, but it didn't get enough upvotes for any responses. Maybe this is a good place to repost (the part relevant to medical treatment is at the end):

Question: Why don't Claude or ChatGPT get message timestamps?

Go to any conversation you’ve had with Claude or ChatGPT in their chat apps and ask them what time they sent their last message, and you’ll find they don’t see timestamps for any of the messages in your chat history. They might guess or hallucinate an answer but they’ll be wildly off and if you probe further they should admit they don’t see timestamps at all, they only know the current time. I’ve also noticed generally that if you pick up conversations over multiple days, it doesn’t know that days have passed in between messages.

——

Me: How much time has passed since your last message?

Claude: I don't have a way to measure the gap between my messages—I don't have a clock running between turns, and nothing in our conversation timestamps the messages for me. So I can't tell you how much time has passed since my last reply. Could've been seconds, could've been days.

If it's useful, I can check the current time, but that would only tell you now, not the interval since I last wrote.

——

This is very bizarre to me and seems like an obvious flaw in the product, but both major AI companies are doing this and not implementing the obvious solution of surfacing message timestamps. Does anyone know why they don’t do this?

For an example of the type of issue this causes, I’ve been using Claude for advice while my dog is going through medical treatment and it’s very concerning because I’ll say I gave him some medicine and then later ask questions about a symptom he’s displaying and Claude doesn’t seem to know whether the medicine was minutes ago or days ago and usually doesn’t ask for clarification either. I’ve learned to either be explicit about how much time has passed or just not ask it questions where timing would be a factor. But I’m surprised because this seems like it should be an easy thing to address.

sentences.en: I’ve also noticed generally that if you pick up conversations over multiple days, it doesn’t know that days have passed in between messages.——Me: How much time has passed since your last message?Claude: I don't have a way to measure the gap between my messages—I don't have a clock running between turns, and nothing in our conversation timestamps the messages for me.
sentences.zh: 我还注意到，如果你接听多天的对话，它并不知道消息之间已经过去了多少天。——我：距离你上一条消息已经过去了多少时间？克劳德：我没有办法衡量我的消息之间的差距——我没有时钟在轮流之间运行，我们的对话中没有任何内容为我的消息添加时间戳。

sentences.en: So I can't tell you how much time has passed since my last reply.
sentences.zh: 所以我无法告诉你自从我上次回复以来已经过去了多长时间。

sentences.en: Could've been seconds, could've been days.If it's useful, I can check the current time, but that would only tell you now, not the interval since I last wrote.——This is very bizarre to me and seems like an obvious flaw in the product, but both major AI companies are doing this and not implementing the obvious solution of surfacing message timestamps.
sentences.zh: 可能是几秒，也可能是几天。如果有用的话，我可以检查当前时间，但这只能告诉你现在，而不是自我上次写信以来的时间间隔。——这对我来说很奇怪，似乎是产品中的一个明显缺陷，但两家主要的人工智能公司都在这样做，而不是实施显示消息时间戳的明显解决方案。

sentences.en: Does anyone know why they don’t do this?For an example of the type of issue this causes, I’ve been using Claude for advice while my dog is going through medical treatment and it’s very concerning because I’ll say I gave him some medicine and then later ask questions about a symptom he’s displaying and Claude doesn’t seem to know whether the medicine was minutes ago or days ago and usually doesn’t ask for clarification either.
sentences.zh: 有谁知道他们为什么不这样做？举个例子，这会导致问题类型，当我的狗接受治疗时，我一直在向克劳德寻求建议，这非常令人担忧，因为我会说我给了他一些药，然后询问有关他表现出的症状的问题，而克劳德似乎不知道药是几分钟前还是几天前，通常也不会要求澄清。

sentences.en: I’ve learned to either be explicit about how much time has passed or just not ask it questions where timing would be a factor.
sentences.zh: 我学会了要么明确说明已经过去了多少时间，要么不问时间因素的问题。

sentences.en: But I’m surprised because this seems like it should be an easy thing to address.
sentences.zh: 但我很惊讶，因为这看起来应该是一件很容易解决的事情。

blocks.html_en: Question: Why don't Claude or ChatGPT get message timestamps?

sentences.en: Question: Why don't Claude or ChatGPT get message timestamps?
sentences.zh: 问题：为什么 Claude 或 ChatGPT 获取不到消息时间戳？

blocks.html_en: Go to any conversation you’ve had with Claude or ChatGPT in their chat apps and ask them what time they sent their last message, and you’ll find they don’t see timestamps for any of the messages in your chat history. They might guess or hallucinate an answer but they’ll be wildly off and if you probe further they should admit they don’t see timestamps at all, they only know the current time. I’ve also noticed generally that if you pick up conversations over multiple days, it doesn’t know that days have passed in between messages.

sentences.en: Go to any conversation you’ve had with Claude or ChatGPT in their chat apps and ask them what time they sent their last message, and you’ll find they don’t see timestamps for any of the messages in your chat history.
sentences.zh: 转到您在 Claude 或 ChatGPT 的聊天应用程序中进行的任何对话，询问他们发送最后一条消息的时间，您会发现他们在您的聊天历史记录中看不到任何消息的时间戳。

sentences.en: They might guess or hallucinate an answer but they’ll be wildly off and if you probe further they should admit they don’t see timestamps at all, they only know the current time.
sentences.zh: 他们可能会猜测或产生幻觉答案，但他们会大错特错，如果你进一步探究，他们应该承认他们根本看不到时间戳，他们只知道当前时间。

sentences.en: I’ve also noticed generally that if you pick up conversations over multiple days, it doesn’t know that days have passed in between messages.
sentences.zh: 我还注意到，如果您在多天内接收对话，它并不知道消息之间已经过了几天。

blocks.html_en: Me: How much time has passed since your last message?

sentences.en: Me: How much time has passed since your last message?
sentences.zh: 我：距离你上次发信息已经过去多久了？

blocks.html_en: Claude: I don't have a way to measure the gap between my messages—I don't have a clock running between turns, and nothing in our conversation timestamps the messages for me. So I can't tell you how much time has passed since my last reply. Could've been seconds, could've been days.

sentences.en: Claude: I don't have a way to measure the gap between my messages—I don't have a clock running between turns, and nothing in our conversation timestamps the messages for me.
sentences.zh: 克劳德：我没有办法测量我的消息之间的差距——我没有在轮流之间运行时钟，而且我们的对话中没有任何内容为我的消息添加时间戳。

sentences.en: So I can't tell you how much time has passed since my last reply.
sentences.zh: 所以我无法告诉你自从我上次回复以来已经过去了多长时间。

sentences.en: Could've been seconds, could've been days.
sentences.zh: 可能是几秒钟，也可能是几天。

blocks.html_en: If it's useful, I can check the current time, but that would only tell you now, not the interval since I last wrote.

sentences.en: If it's useful, I can check the current time, but that would only tell you now, not the interval since I last wrote.
sentences.zh: 如果有用的话，我可以检查当前时间，但这只能告诉你现在，而不是我上次写信以来的时间间隔。

blocks.html_en: This is very bizarre to me and seems like an obvious flaw in the product, but both major AI companies are doing this and not implementing the obvious solution of surfacing message timestamps. Does anyone know why they don’t do this?

sentences.en: This is very bizarre to me and seems like an obvious flaw in the product, but both major AI companies are doing this and not implementing the obvious solution of surfacing message timestamps.
sentences.zh: 这对我来说非常奇怪，似乎是产品中的一个明显缺陷，但两家主要人工智能公司都在这样做，并且没有实施显示消息时间戳的明显解决方案。

sentences.en: Does anyone know why they don’t do this?
sentences.zh: 有谁知道他们为什么不这样做？

blocks.html_en: For an example of the type of issue this causes, I’ve been using Claude for advice while my dog is going through medical treatment and it’s very concerning because I’ll say I gave him some medicine and then later ask questions about a symptom he’s displaying and Claude doesn’t seem to know whether the medicine was minutes ago or days ago and usually doesn’t ask for clarification either. I’ve learned to either be explicit about how much time has passed or just not ask it questions where timing would be a factor. But I’m surprised because this seems like it should be an easy thing to address.

blocks.html_en:
I'm just guessing here, but I find that the models are often quite bad at ignoring information.

So if the harness appended a timestamp to each message the model sees, I wouldn't be surprised if the model often responded with "given that it's 12:30 pm on a Sunday..." even in cases where that's absolutely not relevant.

Maybe you could instead give the model a tool it could call that would return timestamps?

sentences.en: I'm just guessing here, but I find that the models are often quite bad at ignoring information.So if the harness appended a timestamp to each message the model sees, I wouldn't be surprised if the model often responded with "given that it's 12:30 pm on a Sunday..."
sentences.zh: 我只是在这里猜测，但我发现模型通常在忽略信息方面非常糟糕。因此，如果线束为模型看到的每条消息附加时间戳，那么如果模型经常回复“鉴于现在是周日中午 12:30...”，我不会感到惊讶。

sentences.en: even in cases where that's absolutely not relevant.Maybe you could instead give the model a tool it could call that would return timestamps?
sentences.zh: 即使在绝对不相关的情况下。也许您可以为模型提供一个它可以调用并返回时间戳的工具？

Add to that how insurance will randomly deny tests, procedures and treatments, I have very little use for the current medical establishment.

content_html_zh: 我认为，随着患者越来越多地使用人工智能工具对症状和诊断进行自我研究，医生会失去理智，或者至少会变得极度焦躁/沮丧。 我知道这一点是因为我自己也对此感到内疚，最近一位牙医告诉我不要这样做。 这适用于很多卫生工作者、你的全科医生以及你的物理治疗师等。最近，我让克劳德分析了我膝盖的核磁共振成像，它给了我五个不同的潜在问题，但是当我收到放射科医生的报告时——这些问题都不存在，它没有检测到我从放射科医生报告中发现的软骨软化症。健康和法学硕士的当前版本似乎并不匹配。我手头没有参考资料，但参与自己医疗保健的患者会有更好的结果。 此外，对于我们这些对美国医疗保健越来越感到沮丧的人（我们中的很多人）来说（例如，漫长的等待、短暂的访问，你永远没有机会真正有机会讨论健康问题、难以获得适当的测试），人工智能看起来是一个越来越有吸引力的选择。 不是作为医生的替代品，但至少作为一条可能更好地了解正在发生的事情的道路。我喜欢病人研究事物。 当人们关心并试图理解某些东西时，咨询对我来说更有吸引力。尽管如此，患者很难解释医学研究和证据...... ChatGPT 根据对 60 名患者的研究，为我的一些家庭成员推荐了治疗方法。 这并不是完全既定的医疗实践。我当时并没有太认真地研究双子座的一些症状。 它提出了一个我后来忘记的诊断。 我查了一下，人类历史上已知的病例似乎还不到 100 个。我认为有人需要向这些模型传授古老的“马不是斑马”格言，否则我们会有很多人坚持认为患有罕见癌症。成为一名消息灵通、积极参与的患者与“自己研究”的疯狂之间存在着微妙的界限。 很多人不知道那条线在哪里，也不知道他们何时跨越了它。放射学可能是一种边缘情况，因为需要通用多模式语言模型可能缺乏的正确归纳偏差和训练数据。 至少在有人创建 RadiologyBench 并激励实验室将其纳入其数据管道之前。我亲眼目睹了一个非放射学病例（面部性传播疾病），法学硕士做对了，但医生没有做对（他说这与糖尿病有关）。 它可能会挽救那个人的生命，让他们带着法学硕士的诊断去找不同的医生并得到适当的治疗。我不明白的是，为什么医生不使用法学硕士作为在做出决定之前快速获得第二意见的工具？ 他们有我的数据。 他们可以将其放入 GPT 5.6 Pro 或 Fable 中，读取输出，如果他们确实愿意，可以选择忽略它，但如果它有 20% 的机会发现他们错过的东西，为什么不呢？是的。 问题是普通用户不明白法学硕士的视觉能力是有限的。上传放射科医生/病理学家报告并从那里开始与上传原始图像有天壤之别。第一个是个好主意，第二个是鲁莽和危险的。我读到有一个放射科医生的私人软件有更好的训练数据，但我自己无法评论。也许用过它的人可以提供一些反馈。新妈妈能做的最糟糕的事情之一就是使用谷歌，瞬间恐慌发作。 我想使用法学硕士也会做同样的事情。当我的第一个孩子还是婴儿时，任何与打喷嚏相关的信息的谷歌搜索结果都表明这预示着某种超级悲惨的情况。 任何关于行为或特质的建议基本上都会以“你的孩子注定成为连环杀手”结束。对于任何新父母来说，只需打电话给你的医生即可。 为了宝宝的健康，请远离互联网和人工智能。我知道有人手臂上有一个红色肿块，他询问 ChatGPT 这是否是一个问题以及是否应该去看医生。
